From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Greek astronomy](/w/index.php?title=Greek_astronomy&redirect=no "Greek astronomy"))

This article is about ancient Greek astronomy. For pre-scientific cosmology in ancient Greece, see [Early Greek cosmology](/wiki/Early_Greek_cosmology "Early Greek cosmology"). For the practice of astronomy in ancient Babylon, see [Babylonian astronomy](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Antikythera_Fragment_A_%28Front%29.webp/330px-Antikythera_Fragment_A_%28Front%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Antikythera_Fragment_A_(Front).webp)

The [Antikythera mechanism](/wiki/Antikythera_mechanism "Antikythera mechanism") was an [analog computer](/wiki/Analog_computer "Analog computer") designed in the 2nd century BC to calculate the positions of astronomical objects.

**Ancient Greek astronomy** is the [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") written in the [Greek language](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") during [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"). Greek astronomy is understood to include the [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period"), [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), and [late antique](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") eras. Ancient Greek astronomy can be divided into three phases, with *Classical Greek astronomy* being practiced during the 5th and 4th centuries BC, *[Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") astronomy* from the 3rd century BC until the formation of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in the late 1st century BC, and *Greco-Roman astronomy* continuing the tradition in the Roman world. During the Hellenistic era and onwards, Greek astronomy expanded beyond the geographic region of [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") as the Greek language had become the language of scholarship throughout the Hellenistic world, in large part delimited by the boundaries of the [Macedonian Empire](/wiki/Macedonian_Empire "Macedonian Empire") established by [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great"). The most prominent and influential practitioner of Greek astronomy was [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy"), whose *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* shaped astronomical thinking until the modern era. Most of the most prominent constellations known today are taken from Greek astronomy, albeit via the terminology they took on in Latin.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThurston20122-1)

Greek astronomy was influenced heavily by [Babylonian astronomy](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy"), as well as [Egyptian astronomy](/wiki/Egyptian_astronomy "Egyptian astronomy") to a lesser degree. In later centuries, Greek-language astronomical works were translated into other languages, enabling their further spread. Most notably, Arabic translations of these works benefitted astronomers and mathematicians throughout the [Muslim world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world") during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPingree1973-2)

## Key texts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Key texts")]

Many Greek astronomical texts are known only by name, and perhaps by a description or quotations. Some elementary works have survived because they were largely non-mathematical and suitable for use in schools. Books in this class include the *Phaenomena* of [Euclid](/wiki/Euclid "Euclid") and two works by [Autolycus of Pitane](/wiki/Autolycus_of_Pitane "Autolycus of Pitane"). Three important textbooks, written shortly before Ptolemy's time, were written by [Cleomedes](/wiki/Cleomedes "Cleomedes"), [Geminus](/wiki/Geminus "Geminus"), and [Theon of Smyrna](/wiki/Theon_of_Smyrna "Theon of Smyrna"). Books by Roman authors like Pliny the Elder and [Vitruvius](/wiki/Vitruvius "Vitruvius") contain some information on Greek astronomy. The most important primary source is the *Almagest*, since Ptolemy refers to the work of many of his predecessors.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans199824-3)

## Beginnings of Greek astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings of Greek astronomy")]

### Early Greek cosmology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early Greek cosmology")]

Main article: [Early Greek cosmology](/wiki/Early_Greek_cosmology "Early Greek cosmology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Anaximander.jpg/250px-Anaximander.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anaximander.jpg)

Anaximander

The main features of Archaic Greek cosmology are shared with those found in [ancient Near Eastern cosmology](/wiki/Ancient_Near_Eastern_cosmology "Ancient Near Eastern cosmology"). They include (a [flat](/wiki/Flat_Earth "Flat Earth")) Earth, a Heaven (firmament) where the Sun, Moon, and stars are located, an outer ocean surrounding the inhabited human realm, and the netherworld ([Tartarus](/wiki/Tartarus "Tartarus")), the first three of which corresponded to the gods [Ouranos](/wiki/Ouranos "Ouranos"), [Gaia](/wiki/Gaia "Gaia"), and [Oceanus](/wiki/Oceanus "Oceanus") (or [Pontos](/wiki/Pontus_(mythology) "Pontus (mythology)")).[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClay1992132-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarinatos2010196-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimon-Shoshan200870–71-6)

### Ionian school

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ionian school")]

Main article: [Ionian school (philosophy)](/wiki/Ionian_school_(philosophy) "Ionian school (philosophy)")

[Thales of Miletus](/wiki/Thales_of_Miletus "Thales of Miletus") was a primary figure of the Ionian school of Greek philosophy. He is also usually credited for initiating the tradition of [Greek science](/wiki/Science_in_classical_antiquity "Science in classical antiquity") in modern historiography, although others have challenged this attribution.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Like his predecessors, such as [Hesiod](/wiki/Hesiod "Hesiod") and [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer"), Thales accepted that the Earth was [flat](/wiki/Flat_Earth "Flat Earth") and rests on a primordial and endless ocean. He also notably proposed that the universe was fundamentally composed of water.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennettShostakSchneiderMacGregor201716-8) The most famous successors of the tradition begun by Thales were [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") and [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"); while much thought continued to rely on intuition, the lasting legacy of this work was that it offered non-supernatural explanations for the normal operations of the universe; [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") (especially [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry")) was significantly developed and applied on the problems that were worked on; and it was thought that observation could disqualify candidate explanations for how the world worked.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennettShostakSchneiderMacGregor201716–17-9)

[Anaximander](/wiki/Anaximander "Anaximander"), a student of Thales and another prominent member of the Ionian school, realized that the northern sky seems to turn around the [North star](/wiki/North_star "North star"), which led him to the concept of a [Celestial sphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere") around Earth. And, as the sky seems to vary with latitude, he also considered that Earth's surface may be curved as well. However, he incorrectly thought that the Earth was a cylinder as opposed to a sphere. The notion of a spherical Earth first found an audience with the [Pythagoreans](/wiki/Pythagoreanism "Pythagoreanism"), but this was due to philosophical as opposed to scientific reasons: the [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere") was considered a perfectly geometrical figure.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennettShostakSchneiderMacGregor201717-10)

## Framework

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Framework")]

### Axioms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Axioms")]

According to [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") in his [Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest") (1.2), Greek astronomy was predicated on the following assumptions (or *hypotheses* in Greek terminology):[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontelle20209-11)

* The heavens are spherical
* The Heavenly Sphere rotates
* The Earth is spherical
* The Earth is the center of the cosmos
* "the Earth in size and distance has the ratio of a point to the sphere of the fixed stars"
* The Earth is immobile

The first book of the Almagest included a chapter dedicated to the defense of each of these assumptions and refuting alternative positions, using both philosophy and astronomical observation.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontelle20209–10-12)

### Planets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Planets")]

Main article: [Classical planet](/wiki/Classical_planet "Classical planet")

The term "planet" comes from the Greek term [πλανήτης](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%BB%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%AE%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82#Ancient_Greek "wikt:πλανήτης") (*planētēs*), meaning "wanderer", as ancient astronomers noted how certain points of lights moved across the sky in relation to the other stars (which appear fixed). Five planets can be seen with the naked eye: [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"), [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), and [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn"), the Greek names being Hermes, Aphrodite, Ares, Zeus and Cronus.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans20221–7-13) Early Greek astronomers thought that the evening and morning appearances of Venus represented two different objects, calling it *[Hesperus](/wiki/Hesperus "Hesperus")* ("evening star") when it appeared in the western evening sky and *[Phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus_(morning_star) "Phosphorus (morning star)")* ("light-bringer") when it appeared in the eastern morning sky. They eventually came to recognize that both objects were the same planet. Credit is variously attributed to [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras") or [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides") for this discovery.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss2020163-14)

### Constellations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Constellations")]

[Eudoxus](/wiki/Eudoxus_of_Cnidus "Eudoxus of Cnidus") is typically thought to have standardized the names of the constellations. The earliest extant description of the constellations, the *Phaenomena* of [Aratus](/wiki/Aratus "Aratus") (270 BC), is the primary source for his work on this subject. The seventh and eighth books of the Almagest would constitute a star catalogue of the names, positions, and magnitudes of over a thousand stars that Ptolemy placed into the traditional classification of 48 constellations. The most important of these were the twelve constellations that defined the [zodiac](/wiki/Zodiac "Zodiac").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontelle202011-15)

### Sizes of astral bodies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sizes of astral bodies")]

[Aristarchus](/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos "Aristarchus of Samos") also wrote a book *[On the Sizes and Distances of the Sun and Moon](/wiki/Aristarchus_On_the_Sizes_and_Distances "Aristarchus On the Sizes and Distances")*, which is his only work to have survived. In this work, he calculated the sizes of the Sun and Moon, as well as their distances from the Earth in terms of [Earth radii](/wiki/Earth_radius "Earth radius"). Shortly afterwards, [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") calculated the size of the Earth, providing a value for the Earth radii with 252,000 [stades](/wiki/Stadion_(unit) "Stadion (unit)"), which may be equivalent to 39,690 kilometers, rather close to the true figure of 40,120 kilometers.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClagett195592–93-16) [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") wrote another book *[On the Sizes and Distances of the Sun and Moon](/wiki/Hipparchus_On_Sizes_and_Distances "Hipparchus On Sizes and Distances")*, which has not survived. Both Aristarchus and Hipparchus drastically underestimated the distance of the Sun from the Earth.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeugebauer1975325–327-17)

### Geocentrism and heliocentrism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geocentrism and heliocentrism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Aristarchus_working.jpg/250px-Aristarchus_working.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aristarchus_working.jpg)

Aristarchus's 3rd-century BCE calculations on the relative sizes of (from left) the Sun, Earth and Moon, from a 10th-century CE Greek copy

[Geocentrism](/wiki/Geocentrism "Geocentrism"), the idea that the Earth was at the center of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") (or even cosmos) and that the other heavenly bodies, including the Sun, Moon, and the planets revolved around it, was dominant in ancient Greece and ancient cosmographical systems more generally. However, various alternatives appeared at one time or another. For example, the [Pythagorean astronomical system](/wiki/Pythagorean_astronomical_system "Pythagorean astronomical system"), as proposed by [Philolaus](/wiki/Philolaus "Philolaus") in the 5th century BC, proposed that there was an unseen "Central Fire" (not to be confused with the Sun) around which all other bodies of the cosmos revolved.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrosu201954-18) [Heraclides Ponticus](/wiki/Heraclides_Ponticus "Heraclides Ponticus") posited a geo-heliocentric system, where the Sun rotated around the Earth, but all other bodies rotated around the Sun.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrosu201957-19) Finally, in the 3rd century BCE, [Aristarchus of Samos](/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos "Aristarchus of Samos") (sometimes called the "Ancient [Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus")"[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeath1913-20)) was the first and only premodern figure to propose a truly [heliocentric](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") model of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"), placing the Sun, not the Earth, at the center of the universe.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrosu201957–58-21)

## Classical Greek astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Classical Greek astronomy")]

[Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") and [Eudoxus of Cnidus](/wiki/Eudoxus_of_Cnidus "Eudoxus of Cnidus") were both active in astronomical thought in the first half of the fourth century BC, and with them came a decisive shift in Greek astronomy. The work of these two figures represents a shift from earlier stellar concerns, focusing on the study of the stars, to the study of the planet. A new two-sphere model of the Solar System was proposed, and, for the first time, explanations for planetary observations were posited in the form of geometric theories.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindberg201086-22) The two-sphere model posits that Heaven and Earth are a pair of concentric spheres. That is to say, that both Heaven and Earth are conceived of as a sphere which have the same center.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindberg201086-22) In this way, they resemble the structure of an (conceptually spherical) egg, with an outer sphere (the Heaven) encompassing an inner sphere (the Earth).[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBailey1943135–136-23) The outer, celestial sphere contains the fixed stars as well as the Sun, Moon, and planets moving along its surface. The inner terrestrial sphere is fixed in the center. Out of this arises the concept of a "[celestial equator](/wiki/Celestial_equator "Celestial equator")", which is the same as the equator of the Earth, projected outward onto the celestial sphere. The term "[ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic")" refers to the annual path of the Sun around the celestial sphere. This path is tilted 23° with respect to the celestial equator. The two locations where the ecliptic and the celestial equator meet represent the equinoxes (in the [spring](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox") and [fall](/wiki/September_equinox "September equinox")). The two points where the ecliptic is furthest away from the equator represents the [solstices](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice") ([summer](/wiki/Summer_solstice "Summer solstice") and [winter](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice")).[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindberg201086–87-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Ptolemaicsystem-small.png/250px-Ptolemaicsystem-small.png)](/wiki/File:Ptolemaicsystem-small.png)

Renaissance woodcut illustrating the two-sphere model.

[Eudoxus of Cnidus](/wiki/Eudoxus_of_Cnidus "Eudoxus of Cnidus") lived and practiced astronomy in the first half of the fourth century BC. His works are lost and so information about him comes from secondary references in ancient texts. There is a group of fragments about astronomy in the fourth century BC known as the [Eudoxus Papyrus](/w/index.php?title=Eudoxus_Papyrus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eudoxus Papyrus (page does not exist)"), but it contains little relevant informations about the views of Eudoxus himself.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeugebauer1975675–676-25) According to [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") in his commentary on [Aratus](/wiki/Aratus "Aratus"), Eudoxus is thought to have written include one called *Mirror* and another called *Phaenomena*, though an *Oktaeteris* is pseudonymously attributed to him.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeugebauer1975676-26) Another work, *On Speeds*, endeavored to understand the then-unpredictable motions of the planets.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz202285-27) He began his work in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), he went on to found a school in [Cyzicus](/wiki/Cyzicus "Cyzicus") where he gained his reputation. His pupils include [Menaechmus](/wiki/Menaechmus "Menaechmus"), credited as the inventor of the notion of conic sections, and [Polemarchus](/wiki/Polemarchus "Polemarchus"), whose own pupil [Callippus](/wiki/Callippus "Callippus") offered well-received modifications of the Eudoxan theory of homocentric spheres. He also contributed to the calendar and the [parapegma](/wiki/Parapegma "Parapegma") literature.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeugebauer1975676-26)

Eudoxus' model of planetary motion survives as summarized by [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") (*[Metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics_(Aristotle) "Metaphysics (Aristotle)")* XII, 8) as well as the commentary of [Simplicius](/wiki/Simplicius_of_Cilicia "Simplicius of Cilicia") on the *De caelo* of Aristotle, produced in the 6th century AD.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeugebauer1975677-28) Eudoxus' model attempted to explain the planetary motions being observed. The key means by which it did so was by saying the fixed stars were moved along one rotating sphere, whereas each of the planets moved along several nested rotated spheres each with their own speed and pole. Eudoxus established a school of thought that prioritized the use of geometrical models to explain the apparent paths of the stars.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz202285-27) Some, however, noticed flaws in the system of Eudoxus. [Autolycus of Pitane](/wiki/Autolycus_of_Pitane "Autolycus of Pitane") observed that the Moon would be observed to be a different size when the observation was taken at different times. However, this contradicted the Eudoxan theory of homocentrics, since it did not allow for any variation in the distance between the Earth and the Moon.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWildberg1988122-29)

## Hellenistic astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hellenistic astronomy")]

### Apollonius of Perga

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Apollonius of Perga")]

Main article: [Apollonius of Perga](/wiki/Apollonius_of_Perga "Apollonius of Perga")

Apollonius of Perga (c. 240 BCE – c. 190 BCE) responded to the problems in earlier astronomical theories, especially that of Eudoxus, by producing the theory of eccentrics and epicycles (and their deferents). This was further elaborated on by [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") in the second century BC and, later, by [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") in the 2nd century AD. This model allowed for theory to account for changes in the distance between the Earth and other astral bodies.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWildberg1988122-29) However, while Apollonius is often credited with developing this theory, some think that the evidence for this is tenuous. Some evidence may tie in an earlier author, [Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes"), with knowledge of epicycles and eccentrics, and the [Antikythera mechanism](/wiki/Antikythera_mechanism "Antikythera mechanism") also appears to presuppose eccentrics and epicycles in the way it produces calculations.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022319–322-30)

### Hipparchus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hipparchus")]

Main article: [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus")

Hipparchus was a substantial figure of Greek astronomy in the 2nd century BC. He compiled a [star catalogue](/wiki/Star_catalogue "Star catalogue"), according to [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder") observed a *[nova](/wiki/Nova "Nova")* (new star), and discovered the [precession of the equinoxes](/wiki/Precession_of_the_equinoxes "Precession of the equinoxes"). He appears to have had substantial information about [Babylonian astronomers](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy"); no indications of such knowledge of Babylonian astronomy exists for previous Greek authors.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022324-31) It is not known how he had access to this information[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022327-32) and it is likely that knowledge of Babylonian astronomy among the successors of Hipparchus in later eras, such as Ptolemy, relied on Hipparchus for their information of it.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022329-33) Hipparchus' observations allowed him to discover that the [tropical year](/wiki/Tropical_year "Tropical year") was slightly less than 365.25 days, whereas the [sidereal year](/wiki/Sidereal_year "Sidereal year") was slightly more than 365.25 days. Hipparchus is now known to have been correct, although it is not clear how Hipparchus discovered this.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022328–329-34)

## Ptolemaic astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ptolemaic astronomy")]

Main article: [Ptolemaic astronomy](/wiki/Ptolemaic_astronomy "Ptolemaic astronomy")

### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Overview")]

[Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Claudius_Ptolemy "Claudius Ptolemy") was a mathematician who worked in the city of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") in [Roman Egypt](/wiki/Aegyptus_(Roman_province) "Aegyptus (Roman province)") in the 2nd century AD, deeply examining the shape and motion of the Earth and other celestial bodies. Ptolemy's most important work was the *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* (also known as the *Mathematical Composition*) and he composed other works such as the *Hypotheses*, *[Tetrabiblos](/wiki/Tetrabiblos "Tetrabiblos")*, *Handy Tables*, the *Canobic Inscription*, and other minor works.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022360–366-35)

### The *Almagest*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The Almagest")]

Main article: [Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")

The *Almagest* is one of the most influential books in the history of Western astronomy, The *Almagest* was a monumental series of 13 books including roughly a quarter-million words in Greek that gave a comprehensive treatment of astronomy until its time, incorporating theorems, models, and observations from many previous mathematicians.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022366-36) The topics covered by the 13 books are as follows:[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022366–368-37)

* Book 1 explains the presuppositions and tools that he is working with.
* Book 2 provides the basic results that someone can arrive at with a spherical astronomy.
* Book 3 provides a theory of the Sun.
* Book 4 provides an equivalent treatment for the Moon.
* Book 5 deals with the new complications that arise from applying Ptolemy's theory to the Moon, as opposed to the simpler case of the Sun.
* Book 6 combines the theory of the Sun and the Moon to produce a theory that predicts eclipses.
* Books 7 and 8 start freshly; they lay out the theory and practice when working with fixed stars and conclude with a catalogue of 1,022 stars.
* Books 9 to 13 are dedicated to the five visible (and thus, at the time, the five *known*) planets.
* Book 9 lays out a general approach for all the planets, followed by the theory for [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)").
* Book 10 deals with [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") and [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars").
* Book 11 deals with [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") and [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn").
* Book 12 deals with the phenomena of retrogradation and other features of planetary motion.
* Book 13 deals with deviations of the planets from the ecliptic.

### Eccentrics and epicycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Eccentrics and epicycles")]

The Greeks sought to explain how a model could explain the irregular motions of the heavenly bodies. Since the moon and other objects appear to change in size depending on the time of observation, it was understood that the Earths distance to other astral bodies was changing, and that a simple circular motion of another body around the Earth, as in the homocentric theory of Eudoxus, was unable to account for this. Ptolemy accepted and elaborated on the notion of [eccentrics](/wiki/Eccentric_(mechanism) "Eccentric (mechanism)") and [epicycles](/wiki/Deferent_and_epicycle "Deferent and epicycle") to explain this phenomenon. The eccentric is the posit that the observer is not located at the center of rotation. Therefore, if the Earth were not, for example, at the center of the rotation of the Earth, the Moon would appear to have a nonuniform motion to an observation from the Earth: when the Moon was passing by closer to the Earth, its motion would seem faster and it would look larger (because it was closer); otherwise, it would appear slower and smaller. The notion of an epicycle was to say that there was a circle of rotation around the Earth, but to reject the idea that the rotating body itself would be placed on that circle. Instead, a smaller rotating circle would be placed on the larger circle rotating around the Earth, and this smaller circle is called a deferent. The body itself rotates around the circle of the deferent, meanwhile the deferent as a whole would be rotating around the Earth. This would also enable an observer from the Earth to observe an irregular motion on the part of the astral body.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022318–319-38)

Eccentrics and epicycles are the two main tools of Ptolemaic astronomy, and Ptolemy demonstrated that the two were closely related. In the case of the Sun, Ptolemy understood that its motion could be predicted either by an eccentric or by an epicycle.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENetz2022319-39) Once celestial bodies other than the Sun were introduced to the model, such as the planets, it became more complex. The models for Jupiter, Saturn, and Mars included the center of the circle, the equant point, the epicycle, and an observer from Earth to give perspective. The discovery of this model was that the center of the Mercury and Venus epicycles must always be colinear with the Sun. This assures of bounded elongation.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowlerMorus201048-40) Bounded elongation is the angular distance of celestial bodies from the center of the universe. Ptolemy's model of the [cosmos](/wiki/Cosmos "Cosmos") and his studies landed him an important place in history in the development of modern-day science. In the Ptolemaic system, the Earth was at the center of the universe with the Moon, the Sun, and five planets circling it. The circle of fixed stars marked the outermost sphere of the universe and beyond that would be the philosophical "aether" realm. The Earth was at the exact center of the cosmos. The sphere carrying the Moon is described as the boundary between the corruptible and changing sublunary world and the incorruptible and unchanging Heavens above it.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowlerMorus201026-41)

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Influence")]

### Reception of Ptolemy's *Almagest*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Reception of Ptolemy's Almagest")]

Ptolemaic astronomy became standard in medieval western European and [Islamic astronomy](/wiki/Islamic_astronomy "Islamic astronomy") until it was displaced by [Maraghan](/wiki/Maragheh_observatory "Maragheh observatory"), [heliocentric](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") and [Tychonic systems](/wiki/Tychonic_system "Tychonic system") by the 16th century.

The first critical discussion of the Almagest is by [Artemidorus](/w/index.php?title=Artemidorus_(commentator_on_the_Almagest)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Artemidorus (commentator on the Almagest) (page does not exist)") in the late second or early third century, though he understood it poorly. In the fourth century, [Pappus of Alexandria](/wiki/Pappus_of_Alexandria "Pappus of Alexandria") and [Theon of Alexandria](/wiki/Theon_of_Alexandria "Theon of Alexandria") composed commentaries or treatises on sections of the Almagest.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPingree199475–78-42) These works, however, only sought to understand the Almagest as opposed to improving or building upon it. This changed in the fifth century with the [Neoplatonist](/wiki/Neoplatonism "Neoplatonism") philosopher [Proclus](/wiki/Proclus "Proclus"). His exposition of the Almagest displayed, unlike his predecessors, a detailed grasp of the technical details of Ptolemy's work. Though Proclus criticized some elements of the Almagest, such as its suggestion of the existence of epicycles, he and future Neoplatonists believed astronomy was essential to theology and continued to read Ptolemy's works. Students and successors of Proclus to continue working in the tradition of the Almagest included [Hilarius of Antioch](/w/index.php?title=Hilarius_of_Antioch&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hilarius of Antioch (page does not exist)") and Marinus. An ill-studied full-scale commentary on the Almagest was produced in the sixth century, and of interest to historians are the significant number of [scholia](/wiki/Scholia "Scholia") to its margins and between columns by scribes copying the text in later centuries that further engage with the Almagest. The author of the original commentary is, however, not known, as many plausible candidates studied in the astronomy of Ptolemy lived in this era, such as [Eutocius of Ascalon](/wiki/Eutocius_of_Ascalon "Eutocius of Ascalon") and [John Philoponus](/wiki/John_Philoponus "John Philoponus").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPingree199478–95-43)

### Indian astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Greek_astronomy&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Indian astronomy")]

Further information: [Indian astronomy](/wiki/Indian_astronomy "Indian astronomy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/AiKhanoumSunDial.jpg/250px-AiKhanoumSunDial.jpg)](/wiki/File:AiKhanoumSunDial.jpg)

Greek equatorial [sun dial](/wiki/Sun_dial "Sun dial"), [Ai-Khanoum](/wiki/Ai-Khanoum "Ai-Khanoum"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") 3rd-2nd century BCE.

Several Greco-Roman astrological treatises are also known to have been imported into India during the first few centuries of our era. The *[Yavanajataka](/wiki/Yavanajataka "Yavanajataka")* ("Sayings of the Greeks") was translated from Greek to Sanskrit by [Yavanesvara](/wiki/Yavanesvara "Yavanesvara") during the 2nd century, under the patronage of the [Western Satrap](/wiki/Western_Satrap "Western Satrap") [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka") king [Rudradaman I](/wiki/Rudradaman_I "Rudradaman I"). Rudradaman's capital at Ujjain "became the Greenwich of Indian astronomers and the Arin of the Arabic and Latin astronomical treatises; for it was he and his successors who encouraged the introduction of Greek horoscopy and astronomy into India."[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPingree1963-44)

Later in the 6th century, the *[Romaka Siddhanta](/wiki/Romaka_Siddhanta "Romaka Siddhanta")* ("Doctrine of the Romans"), and the *[Paulisa Siddhanta](/wiki/Paulisa_Siddhanta "Paulisa Siddhanta")* (sometimes attributed as the "Doctrine of [Paul](/wiki/Paulus_Alexandrinus "Paulus Alexandrinus")" or in general the Doctrine of Paulisa muni) were considered as two of the five main astrological treatises, which were compiled by [Varahamihira](/wiki/Varahamihira "Varahamihira") in his *Pañca-siddhāntikā* ("Five Treatises").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert2024-45)
